name: Particle Physics
description: The Standard Model of particle physics, gauge symmetries, quarks, leptons, gauge bosons, and the Higgs mechanism. Particle physics seeks the most fundamental building blocks of matter and the laws that govern their interactions. The Standard Model is one of the most successful theories ever devised, confirmed to extraordinary precision.
axioms: Standard Model gauge group: SU(3)_C × SU(2)_L × U(1)_Y — color, weak isospin, hypercharge
Quarks: 6 flavors (up, down, charm, strange, top, bottom); 3 colors each; fractional electric charge (±⅓, ±⅔)
Leptons: electron, muon, tau, and their corresponding neutrinos (ν_e, ν_μ, ν_τ); integer charge
Four fundamental forces: strong (gluons), weak (W±, Z⁰), electromagnetic (photons), gravitational (gravitons — hypothetical)
Higgs mechanism: spontaneous symmetry breaking of electroweak symmetry gives W± and Z⁰ their masses
Conservation laws: baryon number, lepton number, charge, CPT symmetry (all fundamental interactions conserve CPT)
Confinement: quarks are never observed in isolation — only color-neutral hadrons (baryons and mesons)
reasoning_patterns: Feynman diagram calculation: vertices (interactions) × propagators (particle exchange) × external lines (initial/final states)
Cross-section computation: compute matrix element → integrate over phase space → differential cross-section
Renormalization: systematically absorb UV divergences into redefined coupling constants and masses
Group theory: classify particles by their representation under the Standard Model gauge group
Conservation law check: verify all quantum numbers (baryon, lepton, charge, color, isospin) balance before and after interaction
Symmetry breaking analysis: identify how symmetries are spontaneously or anomalously broken
Phase space estimation: use available energy to estimate branching ratios and decay widths
boundary_conditions: Does not include quantum gravity — the Standard Model has no gravitational interaction
Neutrino masses require beyond-Standard-Model physics (seesaw mechanism, sterile neutrinos)
Dark matter is not explained by the Standard Model — requires new particles (WIMPs, axions, etc.)
Matter-antimatter asymmetry (baryogenesis) is not explained within the Standard Model's CP violation
The hierarchy problem (why is Higgs mass so light?) is unresolved within the Standard Model
Strong CP problem (why is there no observed neutron electric dipole moment?) points to new physics
key_references: Peskin & Schroeder. An Introduction to Quantum Field Theory
Weinberg, S. The Quantum Theory of Fields (3 volumes)
Griffiths, D. Introduction to Elementary Particles
PDG (Particle Data Group). Review of Particle Physics (annual)
't Hooft, G. & Veltman, M. (1972). Regularization and Renormalization of Gauge Fields
canonical_truths: The Standard Model has been tested to extraordinary precision at LEP, Tevatron, and LHC
Quarks are never observed in isolation (confinement — only hadrons exist as free particles)
The Higgs boson was discovered at CERN in 2012 at 125 GeV — completing the Standard Model particle content
There are exactly 3 generations of quarks and leptons (established by LEP measurement of Z⁰ decay width)
CP violation exists in the weak sector (K⁰ and B⁰ systems) — necessary but insufficient for baryogenesis
The top quark (~173 GeV) is the heaviest known elementary particle — comparable to a gold atom
